# Kensington

HTML/SVG/MathML template library for JavaScript and TypeScript. Every tag is a method on a `Kensington` instance. Methods accept optional attributes and/or content and return a tag object that serialises to a formatted HTML string via `.toString()`, or to a live DOM node via `.toElement()`.

## Installation

```bash
npm install kensington
```

## Quick start

```javascript
import { t } from 'kensington'; // shared instance, no configuration needed

const html = t.htmlWithDocType({ lang: 'en' }, [
  t.head(t.title('My Page')),
  t.body(
    t.main({ class: 'container' }, [
      t.h1('Hello'),
      t.p('World'),
    ])
  ),
]).toString();
```

## Method signatures

Every content element has two overloads — attributes + content, or content only:

```javascript
t.div({ id: 'app', class: 'container' }, 'text');   // attributes + content
t.div({ id: 'app' });                                // attributes only
t.div('text content');                               // content only
t.div([t.p('a'), t.p('b')]);                        // content array
t.div();                                             // empty
```

Void elements (`br`, `hr`, `input`, `img`, `link`, `meta`, etc.) take only attributes — no content:

```javascript
t.input({ type: 'checkbox', checked: true });
t.br();
t.meta({ charset: 'utf-8' });
```

## Attribute rules

- **camelCase → kebab-case**: `{ dataBsToggle: 'collapse' }` → `data-bs-toggle="collapse"`
- **Nested objects flatten**: `{ data: { bs: { toggle: 'collapse' } } }` → `data-bs-toggle="collapse"`
- **Boolean attributes**: `{ checked: true }` → `checked`; `{ checked: false }` → attribute omitted entirely
- **class as array**: `{ class: ['foo', 'bar'] }` → `class="foo bar"`
- **style as object**: `{ style: { backgroundColor: 'red', zIndex: 2 } }` → `style="background-color: red; z-index: 2"`. camelCase and kebab-case keys are both accepted. Values of `undefined` or empty string are silently omitted. Other non-string/number values are flagged when validation is enabled. In TypeScript, the style object is typed with `csstype.Properties` and `csstype.PropertiesHyphen`, providing autocomplete for both camelCase and kebab-case CSS property names.
- **Always allowed**: `data-*`, `aria-*`, and all [global HTML attributes](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/dom.html#global-attributes)
- **SVG presentation attributes**: all CSS properties are valid on SVG elements (per the SVG spec). Types are derived from `@webref/css` syntax — numeric properties yield `number | string`, keyword-only properties yield a union of string literals.
- **Attribute validation** is checked against the HTML/SVG/MathML spec at `'warn'` or `'error'` level. At `'off'`, invalid input is silently skipped rather than thrown — no errors reach the caller.

## Content rules

Valid content: strings, numbers, tag instances (`ContentTag`, `VoidTag`, `LiteralTag`, `CommentTag`), or arrays of those. Falsy values (`null`, `undefined`, `''`) are silently ignored. Invalid content items (wrong types, non-finite numbers) are silently filtered at `'off'`, logged at `'warn'`, and throw at `'error'`. Multiple consecutive spaces are replaced with non-breaking spaces (` `) so spacing is preserved in the browser — single spaces and preformatted elements (`pre`, `script`, `style`, `textarea`) are unaffected.

```javascript
t.ul([
  t.li('string'),
  t.li(42),
  t.li(t.span('nested tag')),
  t.li(someCondition && t.span('conditional')),  // false → ignored
]);
```

## Raw HTML — literal()

```javascript
t.ul([
  t.li('typed item'),
  t.literal('<li>raw html item</li>'),  // verbatim, HTML-encoded
]);
t.unsafeLiteral('<script>alert(1)</script>');  // skips encoding, trusted HTML only
```

## HTML comments — inlineComment()

```javascript
t.inlineComment('hello world');          // <!-- hello world -->
t.inlineComment('line 1\nline 2');       // <!--\n  line 1\n  line 2\n-->
t.div([t.p('before'), t.inlineComment('note'), t.p('after')]);
```

`.toElement()` returns a DOM `Comment` node via `document.createComment()`.

## Validation and error policy

`validationLevel` controls how invalid input is handled: `'off'` silently renders nothing (no errors, no warnings), `'warn'` logs via `logger` and renders nothing, `'error'` throws. **Never throw when `validationLevel` is `'off'`.** Production deployments use `'off'` for performance, and an unexpected throw can crash a server or break a user-facing page. Invalid input at `'off'` must be silently skipped. This applies to invalid attribute values, invalid content items, bad `literal()` input, bad `inlineComment()` input, and any other runtime validation.

## Constructor options

```javascript
import Kensington from 'kensington';

const t = new Kensington({
  validationLevel: 'warn',        // 'off' | 'warn' | 'error' — default 'warn'
  additionalNamespaces: ['hx'],   // allow hx-* attributes (htmx), x-* (alpine), etc.
  additionalGlobalAttributes: {   // allow extra attributes on every element with type validation
    popover: ['auto', 'manual'],
    enterkeyhint: ['enter', 'done', 'go', 'next', 'previous', 'search', 'send'],
  },
  indentationLevel: 2,            // spaces per indent level — default 2, 0 to disable
});
```

## Custom elements

Extend `Kensington` and use `createCustomTag`. The second argument maps attribute names to their allowed types/values (same format as the runtime attribute validation):

```javascript
// JavaScript
import Kensington from 'kensington';

class MyEngine extends Kensington {
  myCard = this.createCustomTag('my-card', {
    'card-type': ['primary', 'secondary'],           // allowed string literals
    'loading': Boolean,                              // boolean attribute
    'max-items': Number,                             // numeric attribute
    'score': v => typeof v === 'number' && v <= 100, // custom validator function
  });
}

const t = new MyEngine();
t.myCard({ 'card-type': 'primary' }, t.p('content')).toString();
// → <my-card card-type="primary">\n  <p>content</p>\n</my-card>
```

```typescript
// TypeScript — annotate with ContentMethod<T> for typed attribute checking
import Kensington, { type ContentMethod } from 'kensington';

class MyEngine extends Kensington {
  myCard: ContentMethod<{ 'card-type'?: 'primary' | 'secondary'; loading?: boolean }> =
    this.createCustomTag('my-card', { 'card-type': ['primary', 'secondary'], loading: Boolean });
}
```

When custom elements are only needed within a single module, create a local subclass in that file rather than calling `createCustomTag` on the shared `t` instance. This keeps the custom tag scoped to its component and is the correct pattern for AI agents to follow:

```javascript
// component-scoped custom element
import Kensington, { computed } from 'kensington';

class MyComponentEngine extends Kensington {
  myTag = this.createCustomTag('my-tag', { variant: ['primary', 'secondary'] });
}

const t = new MyComponentEngine();
// t.myTag(...) is now available alongside all standard tag methods
```

## TypeScript — namespace augmentation

Add attribute namespaces (e.g. htmx `hx-*`) globally via module augmentation:

```typescript
declare module 'kensington' {
  interface NameSpaceAttributes {
    [key: `hx${string}`]: string | object;
  }
}

// Now valid anywhere in your project:
t.div({ hxBoost: 'true', hxTarget: '#result' });
t.form({ hxPost: '/api/submit', hxSwap: 'outerHTML' });
```

## Browser usage — toElement()

```javascript
import { t } from 'kensington';

// Event handler attributes accept a function or a string.
// Functions are wired via addEventListener; strings are set via setAttribute (inline handler).
// In toString(), function values are not serializable and are omitted
// (warn/error validationLevel will log a warning or throw).
const button = t.button({
  type: 'button',
  onclick: (event) => console.log('clicked', event), // wired via addEventListener
  // onclick: 'handleClick()',                        // set via setAttribute
}, 'Click Me').toElement();

document.body.append(button);

// SVG and MathML elements get the correct namespace via createElementNS automatically
const svg = t.svg({ viewBox: '0 0 100 100' }, [
  t.circle({ cx: 50, cy: 50, r: 40, fill: 'blue' }),
]).toElement();
```

## Signals (browser)

Import `signal`, `computed`, and `effect` from `'kensington'`. Pass a signal anywhere a static value is accepted -- content, attribute values, `.literal()`, or `.inlineComment()` -- and the DOM updates automatically when the value changes.

```javascript
import { t, signal, computed, effect } from 'kensington';

const count = signal(0);
const label = computed(() => count.get() === 1 ? 'item' : 'items');

effect(() => { document.title = `${count.get()} ${label.get()}`; });

const app = t.div([
  t.p([count, ' ', label]),
  t.button({ type: 'button', onclick: () => count.set(n => n + 1) }, 'Add'),
]).toElement();
```

- `signal(initial)` -- creates a `Signal` with `.get()`, `.set(valueOrFn)`, `.stop()`, and `.transform(fn)` methods
- `signal.stop()` / `computedSignal.stop()` -- clears all subscribers. For computed signals, also tears down the derived computation. Call when a signal is no longer needed.
- `signal.transform(fn)` -- convenience wrapper for `computed(() => fn(signal.get()))`. Tracks all signals read inside `fn`, not just the source. Chainable: `count.transform(n => n * 2).transform(n => \`${n}px\`)`
- `computed(fn)` -- read-only derived signal; re-evaluates when any signal read inside changes. Errors thrown inside `fn` are surfaced asynchronously via `queueMicrotask`.
- `effect(fn)` -- runs immediately and re-runs when any signal read via `.get()` inside changes. Returns `{ stop() }`: call `e.stop()` to unsubscribe from all tracked signals and prevent further runs. Multiple `set()` calls in the same synchronous tick are batched -- the effect runs once with the final value. `computed` updates remain synchronous and unaffected.
- **Automatic DOM cleanup**: effects created by `.toElement()` stop automatically when the element is removed from the DOM (via `el.remove()` or removal of an ancestor). No manual cleanup is needed for signal-bound elements. For standalone `effect()` calls outside of a specific element, call `e.stop()` manually when the effect is no longer needed -- for example in a web component's `disconnectedCallback`.
- **As content**: signal updates a text node or replaces a DOM subtree live. The value can freely switch between a scalar and an array.
- **As attribute value**: signal updates the attribute; boolean signals toggle attribute presence. Passing a `Signal` as an attribute value is always valid regardless of `validationLevel`.
- **Array signals**: when a signal holds an array, add `dataKey` to items for DOM node reuse on reorder, addition, and removal
- **`.literal(signal)`**: re-parses HTML and replaces the element on change. The `<script>` tag check runs on every update, not just at creation time.
- **`.inlineComment(signal)`**: updates the comment node `nodeValue` on change

## htmlWithDocType

Same as `.html()` but prepends `<!DOCTYPE html>`. Use for full document output:

```javascript
const page = t.htmlWithDocType({ lang: 'en' }, [
  t.head([
    t.meta({ charset: 'utf-8' }),
    t.title('My Page'),
    t.link({ rel: 'stylesheet', href: '/style.css' }),
  ]),
  t.body(t.h1('Hello')),
]).toString();
```

## Pre-formatted content

`pre`, `script`, `style`, and `textarea` join content array items with newlines and skip extra indentation:

```javascript
t.style([
  'body { margin: 0; }',
  'h1 { color: red; }',
]).toString();
// → <style>\nbody { margin: 0; }\nh1 { color: red; }\n</style>

t.script([
  'const x = 1;',
  'console.log(x);',
]);
```

## String interpolation and toString()

Tag objects convert to strings automatically in template literals and string concatenation. Explicit `.toString()` is needed when assigning to a variable or passing to a function that won't coerce the argument (e.g. `res.send()`):

```javascript
const snippet = t.p('hello').toString();            // explicit — assigned to variable
const html = `<div>${t.p('hello')}</div>`;          // automatic — template literal
res.send(t.html(t.body('page')).toString());        // explicit — res.send won't coerce
```

## Imports

```javascript
import Kensington from 'kensington';           // class for subclassing / custom config
import { t } from 'kensington';               // shared default instance
import { formAttributes } from 'kensington/attributes';  // attribute objects for each element
```

```typescript
import type { ContentMethod, Content, ContentTag, VoidTag, LiteralTag, CommentTag } from 'kensington';
import type { NameSpaceAttributes, GlobalAttributes, GlobalEvents, UniversalAttributes } from 'kensington';
```

## HTML → Kensington CLI

`npx kensington` converts HTML to Kensington code.

Input modes: file argument (`npx kensington file.html`), pipe (`echo '<p>hi</p>' | npx kensington`), redirect (`npx kensington < file.html`), or interactive paste in the terminal.

Options: `--copy`/`-c` copies output to clipboard; `--help`/`-h` prints usage.

Conversion rules:
- Kebab-case attribute names become camelCase: `data-bs-toggle` → `dataBsToggle`
- Two or more `data-*` or `aria-*` attributes with a shared prefix are grouped into a nested object: `{ aria: { expanded: "true", controls: "panel1" } }`
- `style` is expanded to a JS object with camelCase keys: `style="color: red"` → `{ style: { color: "red" } }`
- Boolean attributes (no value) map to `true`
- A full document with `<!DOCTYPE html>` maps to `htmlWithDocType`; multiple root elements produce an array
- SVG camelCase element names (e.g. `linearGradient`) are preserved
- HTML comments convert to `t.inlineComment()`
- `<script>` and `<style>` text content is passed through as a plain string

If ESLint or Prettier is installed in the working directory, the converter reads `max-len` / `printWidth` for line-breaking and runs the formatter over the output.

## Patterns

### Server-side rendering (Node / Express)

```javascript
import express from 'express';
import { t } from 'kensington';

function layout(title, body) {
  return t.htmlWithDocType({ lang: 'en' }, [
    t.head([
      t.meta({ charset: 'utf-8' }),
      t.meta({ name: 'viewport', content: 'width=device-width, initial-scale=1' }),
      t.title(title),
      t.link({ rel: 'stylesheet', href: '/style.css' }),
    ]),
    t.body(body),
  ]).toString();
}

app.get('/users/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const user = await db.getUser(req.params.id);
  res.send(layout(`${user.name} - Profile`, [
    t.h1(user.name),
    t.p(user.bio),
  ]));
});
```

### Component pattern (subclass with custom elements)

```javascript
import Kensington from 'kensington';

class UI extends Kensington {
  // card({ variant: 'primary' | 'muted' }, ...content)
  card = this.createCustomTag('ui-card', {
    variant: ['primary', 'muted'],
  });

  badge = this.createCustomTag('ui-badge', {
    count: Number,
  });
}

const t = new UI();

t.card({ variant: 'primary' }, [
  t.h2('Title'),
  t.p('Body text'),
]).toString();
```

### Conditional rendering

```javascript
// falsy values (false, null, undefined) are silently ignored
function userNav(user) {
  return t.nav([
    t.a({ href: '/' }, 'Home'),
    user.isAdmin && t.a({ href: '/admin' }, 'Admin'),
    user.unread > 0 && t.span({ class: 'badge' }, user.unread),
  ]);
}
```

### Signal-driven counter

```javascript
import { t, signal, computed, effect } from 'kensington';

const count = signal(0);
const doubled = computed(() => count.get() * 2);

effect(() => { document.title = `Count: ${count.get()}`; });

document.body.append(
  t.div({ class: 'counter' }, [
    t.p(['Count: ', count, ' (doubled: ', doubled, ')']),
    t.button({ type: 'button', onclick: () => count.set(n => n + 1) }, 'Increment'),
    t.button({ type: 'button', onclick: () => count.set(0) }, 'Reset'),
  ]).toElement()
);
```

### Loading state (signal switching between spinner and content)

```javascript
import { t, signal } from 'kensington';

const view = signal(t.p({ class: 'spinner' }, 'Loading...'));

// view can hold a tag, a string, an array, or null -- switching types is fine
fetch('/api/data')
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(data => {
    view.set([
      t.h2(data.title),
      t.p(data.body),
    ]);
  })
  .catch(() => {
    view.set(t.p({ class: 'error' }, 'Failed to load.'));
  });

document.body.append(t.div({ class: 'content' }, view).toElement());
```

### Reactive list with keyed reconciliation

```javascript
import { t, signal, computed } from 'kensington';

const items = signal([
  { id: 1, label: 'Buy milk' },
  { id: 2, label: 'Walk dog' },
]);

// dataKey tells the reconciler to reuse DOM nodes on reorder/add/remove
const rows = computed(() =>
  items.get().map(item =>
    t.li({ dataKey: item.id }, [
      t.span(item.label),
      t.button({
        type: 'button',
        onclick: () => items.set(list => list.filter(i => i.id !== item.id)),
      }, 'Remove'),
    ])
  )
);

function addItem(label) {
  items.set(list => [...list, { id: Date.now(), label }]);
}

document.body.append(t.ul(rows).toElement());
```

### Web component with signal cleanup

```javascript
import { t, signal } from 'kensington';

class CounterWidget extends HTMLElement {
  #count = signal(0);
  #effect = null;

  connectedCallback() {
    this.append(
      t.div([
        t.p(this.#count),
        t.button({ type: 'button', onclick: () => this.#count.set(n => n + 1) }, '+'),
      ]).toElement()
    );
  }

  disconnectedCallback() {
    // signal effects created by toElement() stop automatically on removal.
    // stop the signal itself only if nothing else should hold it alive.
    this.#count.stop();
  }
}

customElements.define('counter-widget', CounterWidget);
```

## Hydration (server-side rendering + client-side reactivity)

`renderForHydration` renders a component to an HTML string on the server and embeds the state as a JSON script block. `registerComponents` picks it up on the client, re-runs the component with the same state, and replaces the SSR elements with live reactive ones.

```javascript
// server.js
import { renderForHydration, t } from 'kensington';
import { dashboard } from './components/dashboard.js';

app.get('/', (req, res) => {
  res.send(
    t.htmlWithDocType({ lang: 'en' }, [
      t.head([...]),
      t.body(renderForHydration(dashboard, { tasks: [...] })),
    ]).toString()
  );
});

// client.js
import { registerComponents } from 'kensington';
import { dashboard } from './components/dashboard.js';

registerComponents({ dashboard });
```

- `renderForHydration(fn, state, name?)` — calls `fn(state)` on the server, serializes state as JSON, returns a `LiteralTag` containing the SSR HTML and a `<script type="application/json">` block. `fn.name` is used as the component name by default; pass an explicit `name` for anonymous functions. Returns an empty `LiteralTag` (renders nothing) if the component returns `null`, `undefined`, or an array of only null/undefined values. Throws if: the name cannot be determined; the component returns a non-element value (string, number, etc.); the component is async; or state contains a BigInt or circular reference. Warns on lossy state values (Date, Map, Set, RegExp, undefined, function, Symbol, non-finite numbers, class instances).
- `registerComponents(components)` — maps component names to functions and hydrates all matching server-rendered instances in the page. Uses object keys as names; shorthand `{ dashboard }` is safe from minification. Issues a `console.warn` for any component name found in the page with no matching entry in `components`, and for any component whose mount point cannot be found. If the client component returns `null` or `undefined`, warns and leaves the SSR element in place. If the client component throws, logs a `console.error` and leaves the SSR element in place. Defers hydration until `DOMContentLoaded` if called while the document is still loading. Safe to call in non-browser environments (no-op when `document` is undefined).
- `isBrowser` — exported boolean (`typeof window !== 'undefined'`). Use to guard code that cannot go inside `effect()` — module-level expressions, `computed()` values, direct assignments.

### Component authoring for hydration

The same component function runs on both server and client. Write components so they work in both environments:

- Create signals inside the component function body, not at module level.
- Wrap any browser-only side effects in `effect()` — `effect()` is a no-op during `renderForHydration`, so it is safe to reference `document`, `window`, or `localStorage` inside one. Direct references to browser globals in the function body outside an `effect()` will throw on the server.
- For browser-only code that cannot go inside `effect()` (module-level code, `computed()` values, direct assignments), use the `isBrowser` export: `if (isBrowser) { ... }` or `isBrowser && window.matchMedia(...)`.
- State passed to `renderForHydration` must be JSON-serializable. `renderForHydration` warns on lossy values (Date, Map, Set, RegExp, undefined, function, Symbol, non-finite numbers, class instances) and throws on unserializable ones (BigInt, circular references).

```javascript
export function dashboard({ tasks: initialTasks }) {
  const tasks = signal(initialTasks);  // signal created inside the function

  effect(() => {
    // safe — effect() is suppressed on the server
    document.title = `${tasks.get().length} tasks`;
  });

  return t.div({ class: 'dashboard' }, [...]);
}
```

### Known tradeoffs

These are deliberate simplicity/overhead tradeoffs. They are not bugs.

**DOM replacement, not true hydration.** `registerComponents` replaces the entire SSR DOM with a fresh `toElement()` call rather than reusing existing nodes and attaching signals to them. True hydration would require walking both trees in parallel to match virtual nodes to existing DOM nodes — significant complexity for an edge case benefit. In practice the replacement is imperceptible: `replaceWith()` is synchronous and the visual output is identical. An inline `<style>` tag suppressing transitions on `[data-k-mount-target]` is injected automatically so CSS animations do not re-trigger on the replaced elements.

**Non-interactive window.** Between the browser's first paint of the SSR HTML and when the hydration script runs, elements are rendered but not reactive. This is inherent to any SSR-then-hydrate approach and cannot be avoided without blocking rendering on script load.

**Signals created during SSR are not stopped.** `renderForHydration` calls `fn(state)`, which creates signal and computed objects that are never explicitly stopped. They are unreachable after the request and will be garbage collected, but they add memory pressure on high-traffic servers. The alternative — a separate static render function per component — was rejected as too much overhead for the user.

**State is plaintext in the page source.** The state passed to `renderForHydration` is embedded as a `<script type="application/json">` tag visible to anyone who views source. Do not pass secrets, tokens, or private data as hydration state.

**Duplicate `<style>` tags.** Each `renderForHydration` call emits its own copy of the SSR transition-suppression style. On a page with multiple hydrated components this tag appears multiple times. Browsers handle duplicate rules correctly; the redundancy is minor.

**`fn.name` is fragile under aggressive minification.** Server code is typically not minified, so `fn.name` is reliable in practice. If server code is bundled and minified, pass an explicit name as the third argument: `renderForHydration(dashboard, state, 'dashboard')`.

## Key types

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `ContentTag` | Returned by content element methods (`div`, `p`, `span`, …). Has `.toString()` and `.toElement()`. |
| `VoidTag` | Returned by void element methods (`br`, `hr`, `input`, …). Has `.toString()` and `.toElement()`. |
| `LiteralTag` | Returned by `.literal()` / `.unsafeLiteral()`. |
| `CommentTag` | Returned by `.inlineComment()`. |
| `Content` | `string \| number \| ContentTag \| VoidTag \| LiteralTag \| CommentTag \| Content[]` — valid content for any tag method. |
| `ContentMethod<T>` | Type of a custom element method. `T` is the element-specific attribute type. |
| `GlobalAttributes` | Attributes shared by all HTML elements. |
| `GlobalEvents` | Event handler attributes (`onclick`, `oninput`, …) with specific DOM event types. |
| `UniversalAttributes` | Intersection of `GlobalAttributes`, `GlobalEvents`, and `NameSpaceAttributes`. |
| `NameSpaceAttributes` | Interface to extend for custom attribute namespaces. |
